briefs.ny-stlawrence-massena:
Massena checks out on the fundamentals that matter most for GPU thermals and land flexibility. The 800 acres at $17,223/acre is zoned heavy industrial already, and the climate numbers are strong: 8.6°C ambient and 17.3°C wet-bulb keep free cooling hours high most of the year. Risk scores are clean too, flood zone X, moderate seismic, very low hurricane exposure, and power is priced attractively at $34/MWh given adjacency to NYPA hydro.

The power picture is the softer spot despite the score. Substation headroom of roughly 772 MW is an estimate, not a confirmed allocation, and the ~5.7 year interconnection queue is long for a GPU timeline. Labor and construction sits at 73, the weakest secondary category, which could stretch build schedules in a rural county. On incentives, the sales-tax exemption under §1115(a)(37) is live now but S9288 puts repeal on the table for 2026, so the tax math should be modeled both ways before committing capital.

Advance it to due diligence, but confirm the interconnection timeline and hed
briefs.ny-genesee-stamp: WNY STAMP scores well on the fundamentals that are hard to fix later. Land is strong at 94/100: 1,250 shovel-ready acres, heavy industrial zoning, and $14,538/ac, which is workable for a campus this size. Connectivity (92) and risk (97) are both high, with FEMA flood zone
briefs.ny-oneida-marcy: Marcy scores well on the fundamentals that matter for a GPU campus. Connectivity sits at 97 and risk at
briefs.tx-williamson-taylor:
Taylor Technology Park is in Williamson County (TX, ERCOT) and scores 76 of 100. Samsung Taylor fab corridor; heavy investment in grid and water. Its stronger marks are risk (93) and connectivity (89). The closest substation is about 1.2 km out, with a rough 544 MW of headroom, power near $35 per MWh, and an interconnection wait around 1.9 years.

Summer wet-bulb runs about 24.3°C, which caps how many hours the site can lean on free cooling and shapes its PUE. The softer areas are cooling & climate (36) and power & interconnection (75). FEMA maps it as flood zone X, seismic hazard reads very low, and hurricane exposure is low. TX HB 1223 state sales-tax exemption (10–15 yr) + Chapter 403 / JETI Act property-value limitation (50%, up to 75% in an Opportunity Zone).

Read together, this is a power-favored, warm-climate site. On these numbers it belongs near the top of the diligence list.
briefs.tx-milam-sandow: Sandow Lakes brings a genuinely favorable land package: 1,200 acres at $23,916/ac, zoned heavy industrial, inside a 33k-acre master-planned power district that still carries 345kV infrastructure from the old Alcoa Rockdale smelter. Land score is 90
briefs.ny-niagara-falls: Niagara Power Reach scores well where it counts physically. Land is priced attractively at $16,860/ac across 344 acres, already zoned heavy industrial, and land sc
briefs.ny-montgomery-amsterdam:
Mohawk Valley scores well on the fundamentals that are hard to fix later. Land is cheap at $10,764/ac across 298 acres, zoned industrial, and Risk sits at 97/100 with FEMA flood zone X, low seismic, and very low hurricane exposure. Connectivity (88) and Cooling & Climate (82) are solid too, with a mild 10.2°C ambient and 17.8°C wet-bulb supporting efficient cooling for much of the year. The Thruway corridor location between Albany and Utica gives existing fiber and industrial infrastructure to build from.

Power is the real question. Interconnection scores just 49/100, and while the substation is close (1.2 km, measured), the ~296 MW headroom is only an estimate and the queue wait runs about 5.4 years at $48/MWh. That timeline could stall a GPU buildout regardless of how good the land and risk profile look. Labor & Construction at 68 is middling, and the Opportunity Zone and state data-center sales-tax exemption are currently in force but under legislative review via S9288, so incentive value could shrink.

Advance for diligence, but
briefs.ny-rensselaer-greenbush:
Capital District East scores 72 overall, and the pieces that matter most for durability are solid. Connectivity is 97, land is 81, risk is 97, and cooling looks favorable with an 18.1°C wet-bulb and 10.8°C ambient. The 262-acre parcel is industrial-zoned at $7,766/ac, flood zone X, low seismic, and very low hurricane exposure. Fiber density near Albany and proximity to the NYC latency corridor add real value for connectivity-sensitive workloads.

Power is the weak link. Interconnection scores just 49, with only an estimated ~167 MW of headroom at a substation 1.3 km away, and a ~4.7 year queue at $44/MWh. That timeline could stall any near-term buildout plan. Incentives sit at 56; no Opportunity Zone benefit, and the §1115(a)(37) sales-tax exemption is currently active but under legislative threat from S9288 (Feb 2026), so it shouldn't be modeled as guaranteed.

Advance to next-stage diligence, but treat the interconnection timeline and incentive uncertainty as gating items
briefs.ny-chautauqua-dunkirk:
The Dunkirk parcel scores well outside of power. Connectivity (86) and cooling (83) are strong, with a 17.4°C wet-bulb and 10.5°C ambient giving good hours for economization. Risk is clean: FEMA flood zone X, very low seismic and hurricane exposure. Land is reasonable at 201 acres, $14,713/ac, zoned heavy industrial, and the retained interconnection from the old NRG coal plant is a real asset, only 1.1 km to the substation (measured).

The power picture is the problem. Power & Interconnection sits at 49, driven by an estimated 234 MW of headroom against a roughly 5.1 year interconnection queue at $46/MWh. That queue timeline alone could stall any near-term GPU buildout. The incentives picture is also shaky: the §1115(a)(37) sales-tax exemption applies today but S9288 proposes repeal, so treat that benefit as under legislative review, not locked in.

Advance it for diligence on interconnection timing and incentive status, but do not model this as a near-term build until the queue position and tax treatment firm up.
briefs.ny-franklin-malone:
Malone North Country is in Franklin County (NY, NYISO) and scores 71 of 100. North Country; very cold climate, near hydro-served grid. Its stronger marks are connectivity (99) and risk (92). The closest substation is about 2.1 km out, with a rough 183 MW of headroom, power near $45 per MWh, and an interconnection wait around 4.8 years.

Summer wet-bulb runs about 16.4°C, which caps how many hours the site can lean on free cooling and shapes its PUE. The softer areas are power & interconnection (48) and incentives (56). FEMA maps it as flood zone X, seismic hazard reads moderate, and hurricane exposure is very low. NY §1115(a)(37) internet-data-center sales-tax exemption currently applies but faces proposed repeal (S9288, Feb 2026), so treat it as under legislative review.

Read together, this is a power-constrained, cool-climate site. Reasonable to hold on the shortlist while stronger sites get worked.
briefs.tx-tarrant-alliance: AllianceTexas North scores well on land and location fundamentals. The 353-acre parcel at $16,589/acre carries industrial zoning already
briefs.tx-ellis-ennis:
Ennis North Industrial is in Ellis County (TX, ERCOT) and scores 69 of 100. I-45 corridor south of Dallas; established industrial base and rail. Its stronger marks are risk (97) and incentives (91). The closest substation is about 2.8 km out, with a rough 287 MW of headroom, power near $40 per MWh, and an interconnection wait around 2.9 years.

Summer wet-bulb runs about 24.2°C, which caps how many hours the site can lean on free cooling and shapes its PUE. The softer areas are cooling & climate (39) and power & interconnection (58). FEMA maps it as flood zone X, seismic hazard reads very low, and hurricane exposure is very low. It falls inside a federal Opportunity Zone. TX HB 1223 state sales-tax exemption (10–15 yr) + Chapter 403 / JETI Act property-value limitation (50%, up to 75% in an Opportunity Zone).

Read together, this is a power-constrained, warm-climate site. Reasonable to hold on the shortlist while stronger sites get worked.
briefs.tx-dallas-lancaster:
Lancaster screens well on the fundamentals that matter for uptime. Risk sits at 97, with FEMA flood zone X and very low seismic and hurricane exposure, which keeps insurance and design contingencies simple. Connectivity scores 93, and the substation is only 2.5 km away (measured), a real plus for interconnection routing. Land is 282 acres at $15,526/ac, zoned industrial already, no rezoning fight expected. Incentives are solid too: TX HB 1223 sales-tax exemption plus a 50% Chapter 403/JETI property value limitation, even without Opportunity Zone status.

The soft spots are power and climate. Power & Interconnection is only 58, and the ~286 MW
briefs.tx-nueces-corpus:
Corpus Christi checks several boxes hard. Land is cheap at $19,699/ac across 360 acres, already zoned heavy_industrial, and the substation sits 2 km out with roughly 103 MW of headroom (estimated) at a $34/MWh rate. Connectivity scores 96, the strongest number on the sheet, and incentives are unusually deep: Opportunity Zone status, HB 1223's 10-15 year sales-tax exemption, and a JETI property-value limitation up to 75% given the OZ stacking. Labor and construction come in at 79, suggesting a workable build environment.

The risk side is less comfortable. Power & Interconnection lands at only 58, and the ~1.8 year wait plus an estimated (not measured) headroom figure both deserve confirmation before sizing load. Cooling & Climate is the weak point
briefs.tx-harris-katy:
Katy Prairie West lands a 68 composite, and the connectivity score of 95 is the clear driver, fitting the dense fiber routes into Houston metro on a growth edge northwest of the city. The 312-acre parcel at $15,984/acre is priced reasonably for light-industrial zoning, and risk scoring at 87 looks solid: flood zone X, very low seismic activity per USGS. Incentives help too, with the state sales-tax exemption under HB 1223 and a 50% property-value limitation via Chapter 403/JETI, though this isn't an Opportunity Zone site so the 75% tier doesn't apply.

Power is the softer spot. Interconnection scores 58, and the roughly 3-year wait plus an estimated 306 MW of headroom (not confirmed by the utility) leaves real uncertainty for a GPU load profile. The substation is close, 2.6 km measured, and $41/MWh is workable, but the queue timeline could slip. Cooling is the weakest category at 45. A 25.2°C wet-bulb (derived, not measured on-site) with 22°C ambient means evaporative coo
briefs.tx-hood-comanche: Comanche Peak Corridor scores well where it counts most for underwriting: a 97/100 risk profile, with FEMA flood zone X and very low seismic and hurricane exposure, is hard to find in ERCOT. Connectivity at 87/100 and a measured 2.2 km substation distance are favorable, and the adjac
briefs.tx-bell-temple:
Temple checks out on the fundamentals that are hardest to fix. Land is cheap and plentiful at 366 acres for $16,785/acre, zoned industrial already, and the Risk score of 93 reflects FEMA flood zone X, very low USGS seismic hazard, and low hurricane exposure. Connectivity sits at 83 given the I-35 corridor position between Austin and Waco. Incentives are real: TX HB 1223 sales-tax exemption plus a Chapter 403/JETI property-value limitation of 50%, potentially 75% if it qualifies as an Opportunity Zone (it currently does not).

Power is the softer story. The substation is a measured 2.5 km out, but the ~341 MW headroom is an estimate, and a ~3.2 year interconnection queue is long for anyone targeting near-term GPU deployment. Cooling is the weak point at 38/100; a 24°C wet-bulb and 21.4°C ambient (derived from NOAA/Open-Meteo, not site-measured) mean higher parasitic cooling load than dry-climate sites
briefs.tx-wise-decatur: Decatur screens well on the fundamentals that are hardest to fix later. Risk and incentives both come in above 90: flood zone X, very low seismic and hurricane exposure, an Opportunity Zone designation, plus the HB 1223 sales-tax exemption and Chapter 403/JETI valuation limitation, which could reach 75 percent given the OZ overlap. Connectivity is strong at 87. Land
briefs.tx-bastrop-austin: Bastrop Gateway scores well on the things that matter for uptime and siting ease. Connectivity hits 91 and risk hits 93, with FEMA flood zone X, very low seismic exposure, and low hurricane risk, a rare clean sweep. The substation is 1.7 km away (measured), and zoning is already light industrial across the full 203 acres at $13,662/ac. Incentives are solid too: HB 1223's state
briefs.tx-wharton-elcampo: El Campo Plains has a few things going for it. The substation sits 0.5 km from the parcel with an estimated 223 MW of headroom, and power at $38/MWh is cheap for ERCOT. Connectivity scores 85/100, which is high for a rural Wharton County site. Incentives are the real draw at 91/100: Opportunity Zone status stacks with the HB 1223 sales-tax exemption and a JETI/Chapter 403 valuation limitation that could reach 75%.
